name: Recursive
summary: Recursively process each node: fix the rest of the list first, then decide whether to keep or skip the current node.
visualizationIntent: init: the full linked list is shown; first call enters with head at node 0. recurse: each recursive call advances one node deeper, shown as a new call-stack frame. check: on the way back up, the current node's value is compared to val. update (skip): if it matches val, the node is returned as head.next (skipping it from the visual). return: the surviving node or None is returned to the caller frame.

lineExplanations.3: Store the integer value on this ListNode.
lineExplanations.4: Set the next pointer to None (or the provided node) — None marks the tail.
lineExplanations.8: First-call conversion: if head is still a Python list, convert it to real ListNode objects.
lineExplanations.9: Save the raw integer values from the input list.
lineExplanations.10: Create one ListNode for each value.
lineExplanations.11: Iterate over every node except the last to wire up .next pointers.
lineExplanations.12: Chain node k to node k+1.
lineExplanations.13: Set head to the first node (or None for an empty input).
lineExplanations.14: Call the pure recursive helper _recurse which returns the new head ListNode (or None).
lineExplanations.15: Initialise the result list that will hold surviving values.
lineExplanations.16: Start the result-collection walk at new_head.
lineExplanations.17: Walk the cleaned linked list until we reach None.
lineExplanations.18: Append each surviving node's value to result.
lineExplanations.19: Advance to the next surviving node.
lineExplanations.20: Return the list of surviving values as the answer.
lineExplanations.23: Base case: head is None — we have reached the end of the list.
lineExplanations.24: Return None to signal an empty tail to the caller.
lineExplanations.25: Recurse on head.next: clean the tail first, then come back to handle the current node.
lineExplanations.26: After tail is clean, check if the current node's value equals val.
lineExplanations.27: It matches — skip this node by returning head.next (the already-cleaned tail starts here).
lineExplanations.28: It does not match — keep this node by returning head itself.

syntaxExplanations.1: class ListNode: the linked list node class. val stores the integer; next points to the following node.
syntaxExplanations.2: def __init__: constructor — val is the integer value, nxt is the next-node pointer (default None).
syntaxExplanations.3: self.val = val: stores the integer on the node.
syntaxExplanations.4: self.next = nxt: stores the pointer to the next node.
syntaxExplanations.6: class Solution: the required LeetCode wrapper class.
syntaxExplanations.7: def removeElements(self, head, val): head is the first node (or input list from preset), val is the target.
syntaxExplanations.8: isinstance(head, list): Python's way to check if head is still a plain list (first call only).
syntaxExplanations.9: vals = head: rename for clarity before we build real nodes.
syntaxExplanations.10: [ListNode(v) for v in vals]: list comprehension — one ListNode per integer.
syntaxExplanations.11: for k in range(len(nodes_list) - 1): iterate 0 to second-to-last index.
syntaxExplanations.12: nodes_list[k].next = nodes_list[k + 1]: link each node to the next.
syntaxExplanations.13: head = nodes_list[0] if nodes_list else None: first node becomes head; None if empty.
syntaxExplanations.14: self._recurse(head, val): call the private recursive helper that returns a ListNode, not a list.
syntaxExplanations.15: result = []: empty list to accumulate surviving values.
syntaxExplanations.16: node = new_head: start walking from the new head.
syntaxExplanations.17: while node: Python's way to loop while node is not None.
syntaxExplanations.18: result.append(node.val): add this node's integer to the output list.
syntaxExplanations.19: node = node.next: follow the next-pointer to advance one step.
syntaxExplanations.20: return result: hand back the list of surviving values as the final answer.
syntaxExplanations.21: Blank line separates the two methods.
syntaxExplanations.22: def _recurse(self, head, val): the pure recursive helper that works entirely on ListNodes.
syntaxExplanations.23: if head is None: base case — nothing left to process.
syntaxExplanations.24: return None: signal an empty tail to the calling frame.
syntaxExplanations.25: head.next = self._recurse(head.next, val): recurse into the tail, then reattach the cleaned tail to head.
syntaxExplanations.26: if head.val == val: check the current node AFTER the tail is already clean.
syntaxExplanations.27: return head.next: skip this node — the caller receives the cleaned tail as its next.
syntaxExplanations.28: return head: keep this node — the caller receives head (with its already-cleaned .next) as the local result.
